title: Peptic Ulcer Disease
oneLiner: Erosions in gastric/duodenal mucosa causing epigastric pain ± bleeding; common (H. pylori, NSAIDs), can perforate or haemorrhage.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Perforated ulcer** → free intra-abdominal air → **the single finding: board-like rigidity + rebound tenderness + patient unwilling to move** (may have referred shoulder pain). Needs urgent surgical review.
- **Haemorrhage from ulcer** → haematemesis / melaena → **the single finding: haemodynamic instability (HR >100, SBP <100) with ongoing fresh red haematemesis or massive melaena** (not just coffee-ground). Needs immediate OGD ± transfusion.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Epigastric gnawing or burning pain (often related to meals: duodenal ulcer worse 2-3h after eating/eases with food; gastric ulcer worse with food)
  - Nausea, bloating, early satiety
  - Dyspepsia (indigestion) refractory to antacids
  - Alarm features: weight loss, dysphagia, anaemia, GI bleeding
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Epigastric tenderness (may be mild or absent)
  - If bleeding: tachycardia, postural hypotension, pallor, melaena on PR exam
  - If perforation: generalised guarding, rebound, absent bowel sounds, severe pain
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely -> less likely:**
  - Functional dyspepsia – no alarm features, normal OGD
  - GORD – retrosternal heartburn, worse on lying/bending, responds to acid suppression
  - Biliary colic – right upper quadrant + epigastric pain, worse after fatty meal, Murphy’s sign positive
  - Pancreatitis – epigastric pain radiating to back, vomiting, lipase >3x ULN
  - Gastric cancer – persistent epigastric pain with weight loss, dysphagia, anaemia (older age, Helicobacter pylori, smokers)
  - Superior mesenteric artery syndrome – post-prandial epigastric pain, vomiting, weight loss (thin young women)
- **Splitter:** "Pain that wakes the patient from sleep" – highly specific for duodenal ulcer (vs functional dyspepsia which does not typically wake)
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - ECG (to rule out inferior MI if epigastric pain)
  - Urine dip / pregnancy test (if female of childbearing age)
  - PR exam (check for melaena)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (anaemia, infection), U&E (dehydration, AKI), LFT (exclude biliary/pancreatic), amylase/lipase (pancreatitis), CRP (inflammation)
  - Group and screen / crossmatch (if active bleeding)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Erect CXR (or lateral decubitus) – look for free subdiaphragmatic gas (perforation)
  - **Second-line:** CT abdomen (with IV contrast) if CXR equivocal or complicated disease (perforation, penetration, abscess)
- **Special tests:**
  - Oesophagogastroduodenoscopy (OGD) – gold standard for diagnosis, biopsy for H. pylori (CLO test + histology ± culture), and to treat bleeding
  - H. pylori testing:
    - Pre-OGD: ¹³C urea breath test or stool antigen (not during PPIs/antibiotics/no biopsy)
    - Post-OGD: rapid urease test / histology
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - **Rockall score** (for upper GI bleed): pre-endoscopy (age, shock, comorbidity) + post-endoscopy (stigmata of bleeding) → predicts rebleeding and mortality. Score ≥5 high risk.
  - **Blatchford score** (for need for intervention in UGIB): Urea, Hb, SBP, HR, melaena, syncope, liver disease, cardiac failure. Score >0 indicates need for OGD.
- **Never-miss test:** **Erect CXR** in any patient with epigastric pain + peritonism – missed perforation is lethal.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **If perforation:** IV fluids, nil by mouth, NG tube, urgent surgical review (emergency laparotomy ± repair)
  - **If bleeding:** resuscitate with IV fluids, crossmatch, transfuse if Hb <70 g/L (or <80 if significant comorbidity), IV PPI (omeprazole 80mg loading followed by 8mg/h infusion [check local policy]), urgent OGD (within 1-2h if unstable, within 24h if stable)
  - **If non-bleeding/uncomplicated:** start PPO (oral omeprazole 20-40mg daily), plan outpatient OGD
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - **If bleeding post-OGD:** continue IV PPI for 72h if endoscopic treatment (e.g., clips, adrenaline, thermal), then switch to oral PPI
  - **If H. pylori positive:** start eradication therapy – triple therapy (PPI + amoxicillin 1g BD + clarithromycin 500mg BD) for 7 days (check local resistance patterns; may use metronidazole if allergic to penicillin)
  - **If NSAID-related:** stop NSAID (if possible), consider COX-2 inhibitor less risky but still ulcerogenic – use with PPI
  - **If no H. pylori/no NSAID:** prolonged PPI for 8 weeks, then consider cause (Zollinger-Ellison, Crohn’s, CMV, etc.)
  - **Anaemia management:** iron supplements if iron deficient, consider B12/folate
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **H. pylori:** confirm eradication with breath test or stool antigen (at least 4 weeks after finishing antibiotics, off PPI for 2 weeks)
  - **PPI maintenance:** for high-risk (recurrent ulcer, ongoing NSAID use, severe oesophagitis) – lowest effective dose (e.g., omeprazole 20mg daily)
  - **Lifestyle:** avoid smoking, alcohol, NSAIDs; manage stress
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: IV crystalloid (e.g., Hartmann's) at maintenance rate if nil by mouth; if bleeding, blood transfusion as above
  - VTE prophylaxis: prophylactic LMWH (e.g., enoxaparin 40mg SC daily) unless actively bleeding (then defer)
  - Diet/NBM: **NBM if active bleeding or perforation**; otherwise, soft diet when bleeding stops
  - Observations: 4-hourly vital signs (NEWS2); if bleeding, hourly until stable then 4-hourly; check stool colour daily
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Ulcer healing (non-bleeding):** Omeprazole 20mg PO once daily (or lansoprazole 30mg, pantoprazole 40mg) for 4-8 weeks
  - **Active bleeding:** IV PPI: Omeprazole 80mg loading dose over 30 minutes, then 8mg/h continuous infusion for 72h post-endoscopy [check local policy: may use pantoprazole 80mg loading + 8mg/h]
  - **H. pylori eradication (7 days):** Omeprazole 20mg BD + Amoxicillin 1g BD + Clarithromycin 500mg BD (or Metronidazole 400mg BD if penicillin allergy) [check local formulary; triple therapy for 7 days is standard in UK]
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Lansoprazole 30mg daily / Rabeprazole 20mg daily / Esomeprazole 40mg daily
  - If PPI intolerance: H2RA (e.g., Ranitidine 150mg BD) but less effective for bleeding
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - PPIs: decrease absorption of B12, iron, Ca; increase risk of C. diff, pneumonia, hip fracture with long-term use
  - Clopidogrel + omeprazole: possible interaction (use pantoprazole if needed)
  - Amoxicillin: rash if mononucleosis; allergic reactions
  - Clarithromycin: QT prolongation (caution with other QTc-prolonging drugs); increases warfarin effect
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - PPIs no dose adjustment in renal impairment; caution in severe liver disease (reduce omeprazole dose)
  - Amoxicillin: reduce dose if CrCl <30 (e.g., 500mg BD); clarithromycin: reduce if CrCl <30
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Omeprazole IV infusion – failure to match infusion rate (8mg/h) leads to sub-therapeutic levels** during active bleeding. Also: **Triple therapy – prescribing without checking C. diff risk in elderly** (proton pump + broad-spectrum antibiotics) – use only if H. pylori confirmed.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Haemorrhage (haematemesis/melaena) → hypovolaemic shock, death
  - Perforation → peritonitis, sepsis, multi-organ failure
  - Penetration (into adjacent organs: pancreas, liver) → persistent pain, abscess
  - Gastric outlet obstruction (oedema or scarring) → intractable vomiting, metabolic alkalosis
- **Chronic:**
  - Recurrent ulcer (if H. pylori not eradicated or NSAIDs continued)
  - Gastric cancer (in long-standing H. pylori gastritis) – rare but screen if family history or alarm features
  - Iron deficiency anaemia (chronic oozing)
  - PPI long-term: C. difficile infection, osteoporosis, hypomagnesaemia
sections.Prognosis:
- **Bleeding ulcer:** mortality 5-10% (higher with age, comorbidity, rebleeding). With endoscopic treatment, rebleed rate ~10-20%.
- **Perforated ulcer:** mortality 10-30% (higher if >24h delay, sepsis). Early surgery + antibiotics improves outcome.
- **Non-complicated ulcer:** >90% heal with PPI and H. pylori eradication; recurrence <5% if successful eradication.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always check H. pylori status** in all peptic ulcers – if positive, initiate eradication therapy during the same admission (even if bleeding, provided haemodynamically stable)
- **Perform a PR exam** for melaena in any patient with epigastric pain + anaemia – a negative exam does not exclude upper GI bleed
- **If prescribed NSAID + PPI, still consider stopping the NSAID** – PPI reduces risk but does not eliminate it, especially in high-risk patients (elderly, previous ulcer, concomitant anticoagulant)
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not start empiric H. pylori treatment without confirming infection** (false positives with recent antibiotics/PPIs) and do not use antibiotics alone without PPI
- **Do not stop PPI prematurely** in bleeding ulcer: recommended 72h continuous infusion post-endoscopic haemostasis followed by oral PPI for 8 weeks
- **Do not give NSAIDs or steroids** to a patient with untreated H. pylori ulcer – may precipitate bleeding
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Black/tarry stools or red blood in vomit/stools
  - Severe abdominal pain (unrelieved by PPI, worse with movement)
  - Fever, rigors, vomiting, or inability to tolerate oral fluids/food
- **Follow-up:**
  - Outpatient OGD in 6-8 weeks if not done (or if gastric ulcer to confirm healing and exclude malignancy)
  - H. pylori eradication test at least 4 weeks after completing triple therapy (off PPI for 2 weeks) – arrange breath test or stool antigen
  - GP review if ongoing dyspepsia or alarm features
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **When to call senior / ICU / crash team:**
  - **NEWS2 ≥7** or rising trend with shock (HR >120, SBP <90, RR >24)
  - **Lactate >4 mmol/L** (sign of tissue hypoperfusion)
  - **GCS <13** or altered mental state
  - **Ongoing haematemesis/melaena despite resuscitation** (call ICU + on-call gastroenterology for emergency OGD)
  - **Peritonism or suspected perforation** (call surgical registrar immediately)
  - **Failed endoscopic control of bleeding** (consider interventional radiology or surgery)

rawMarkdown:
## Peptic Ulcer Disease
**One-liner:** Erosions in gastric/duodenal mucosa causing epigastric pain ± bleeding; common (H. pylori, NSAIDs), can perforate or haemorrhage.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Perforated ulcer** → free intra-abdominal air → **the single finding: board-like rigidity + rebound tenderness + patient unwilling to move** (may have referred shoulder pain). Needs urgent surgical review.
- **Haemorrhage from ulcer** → haematemesis / melaena → **the single finding: haemodynamic instability (HR >100, SBP <100) with ongoing fresh red haematemesis or massive melaena** (not just coffee-ground). Needs immediate OGD ± transfusion.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Epigastric gnawing or burning pain (often related to meals: duodenal ulcer worse 2-3h after eating/eases with food; gastric ulcer worse with food)
  - Nausea, bloating, early satiety
  - Dyspepsia (indigestion) refractory to antacids
  - Alarm features: weight loss, dysphagia, anaemia, GI bleeding
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Epigastric tenderness (may be mild or absent)
  - If bleeding: tachycardia, postural hypotension, pallor, melaena on PR exam
  - If perforation: generalised guarding, rebound, absent bowel sounds, severe pain

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely -> less likely:**
  - Functional dyspepsia – no alarm features, normal OGD
  - GORD – retrosternal heartburn, worse on lying/bending, responds to acid suppression
  - Biliary colic – right upper quadrant + epigastric pain, worse after fatty meal, Murphy’s sign positive
  - Pancreatitis – epigastric pain radiating to back, vomiting, lipase >3x ULN
  - Gastric cancer – persistent epigastric pain with weight loss, dysphagia, anaemia (older age, Helicobacter pylori, smokers)
  - Superior mesenteric artery syndrome – post-prandial epigastric pain, vomiting, weight loss (thin young women)
- **Splitter:** "Pain that wakes the patient from sleep" – highly specific for duodenal ulcer (vs functional dyspepsia which does not typically wake)

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - ECG (to rule out inferior MI if epigastric pain)
  - Urine dip / pregnancy test (if female of childbearing age)
  - PR exam (check for melaena)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (anaemia, infection), U&E (dehydration, AKI), LFT (exclude biliary/pancreatic), amylase/lipase (pancreatitis), CRP (inflammation)
  - Group and screen / crossmatch (if active bleeding)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Erect CXR (or lateral decubitus) – look for free subdiaphragmatic gas (perforation)
  - **Second-line:** CT abdomen (with IV contrast) if CXR equivocal or complicated disease (perforation, penetration, abscess)
- **Special tests:**
  - Oesophagogastroduodenoscopy (OGD) – gold standard for diagnosis, biopsy for H. pylori (CLO test + histology ± culture), and to treat bleeding
  - H. pylori testing:
    - Pre-OGD: ¹³C urea breath test or stool antigen (not during PPIs/antibiotics/no biopsy)
    - Post-OGD: rapid urease test / histology
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - **Rockall score** (for upper GI bleed): pre-endoscopy (age, shock, comorbidity) + post-endoscopy (stigmata of bleeding) → predicts rebleeding and mortality. Score ≥5 high risk.
  - **Blatchford score** (for need for intervention in UGIB): Urea, Hb, SBP, HR, melaena, syncope, liver disease, cardiac failure. Score >0 indicates need for OGD.
- **Never-miss test:** **Erect CXR** in any patient with epigastric pain + peritonism – missed perforation is lethal.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **If perforation:** IV fluids, nil by mouth, NG tube, urgent surgical review (emergency laparotomy ± repair)
  - **If bleeding:** resuscitate with IV fluids, crossmatch, transfuse if Hb <70 g/L (or <80 if significant comorbidity), IV PPI (omeprazole 80mg loading followed by 8mg/h infusion [check local policy]), urgent OGD (within 1-2h if unstable, within 24h if stable)
  - **If non-bleeding/uncomplicated:** start PPO (oral omeprazole 20-40mg daily), plan outpatient OGD
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - **If bleeding post-OGD:** continue IV PPI for 72h if endoscopic treatment (e.g., clips, adrenaline, thermal), then switch to oral PPI
  - **If H. pylori positive:** start eradication therapy – triple therapy (PPI + amoxicillin 1g BD + clarithromycin 500mg BD) for 7 days (check local resistance patterns; may use metronidazole if allergic to penicillin)
  - **If NSAID-related:** stop NSAID (if possible), consider COX-2 inhibitor less risky but still ulcerogenic – use with PPI
  - **If no H. pylori/no NSAID:** prolonged PPI for 8 weeks, then consider cause (Zollinger-Ellison, Crohn’s, CMV, etc.)
  - **Anaemia management:** iron supplements if iron deficient, consider B12/folate
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **H. pylori:** confirm eradication with breath test or stool antigen (at least 4 weeks after finishing antibiotics, off PPI for 2 weeks)
  - **PPI maintenance:** for high-risk (recurrent ulcer, ongoing NSAID use, severe oesophagitis) – lowest effective dose (e.g., omeprazole 20mg daily)
  - **Lifestyle:** avoid smoking, alcohol, NSAIDs; manage stress
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: IV crystalloid (e.g., Hartmann's) at maintenance rate if nil by mouth; if bleeding, blood transfusion as above
  - VTE prophylaxis: prophylactic LMWH (e.g., enoxaparin 40mg SC daily) unless actively bleeding (then defer)
  - Diet/NBM: **NBM if active bleeding or perforation**; otherwise, soft diet when bleeding stops
  - Observations: 4-hourly vital signs (NEWS2); if bleeding, hourly until stable then 4-hourly; check stool colour daily

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Ulcer healing (non-bleeding):** Omeprazole 20mg PO once daily (or lansoprazole 30mg, pantoprazole 40mg) for 4-8 weeks
  - **Active bleeding:** IV PPI: Omeprazole 80mg loading dose over 30 minutes, then 8mg/h continuous infusion for 72h post-endoscopy [check local policy: may use pantoprazole 80mg loading + 8mg/h]
  - **H. pylori eradication (7 days):** Omeprazole 20mg BD + Amoxicillin 1g BD + Clarithromycin 500mg BD (or Metronidazole 400mg BD if penicillin allergy) [check local formulary; triple therapy for 7 days is standard in UK]
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Lansoprazole 30mg daily / Rabeprazole 20mg daily / Esomeprazole 40mg daily
  - If PPI intolerance: H2RA (e.g., Ranitidine 150mg BD) but less effective for bleeding
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - PPIs: decrease absorption of B12, iron, Ca; increase risk of C. diff, pneumonia, hip fracture with long-term use
  - Clopidogrel + omeprazole: possible interaction (use pantoprazole if needed)
  - Amoxicillin: rash if mononucleosis; allergic reactions
  - Clarithromycin: QT prolongation (caution with other QTc-prolonging drugs); increases warfarin effect
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - PPIs no dose adjustment in renal impairment; caution in severe liver disease (reduce omeprazole dose)
  - Amoxicillin: reduce dose if CrCl <30 (e.g., 500mg BD); clarithromycin: reduce if CrCl <30
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Omeprazole IV infusion – failure to match infusion rate (8mg/h) leads to sub-therapeutic levels** during active bleeding. Also: **Triple therapy – prescribing without checking C. diff risk in elderly** (proton pump + broad-spectrum antibiotics) – use only if H. pylori confirmed.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Haemorrhage (haematemesis/melaena) → hypovolaemic shock, death
  - Perforation → peritonitis, sepsis, multi-organ failure
  - Penetration (into adjacent organs: pancreas, liver) → persistent pain, abscess
  - Gastric outlet obstruction (oedema or scarring) → intractable vomiting, metabolic alkalosis
- **Chronic:**
  - Recurrent ulcer (if H. pylori not eradicated or NSAIDs continued)
  - Gastric cancer (in long-standing H. pylori gastritis) – rare but screen if family history or alarm features
  - Iron deficiency anaemia (chronic oozing)
  - PPI long-term: C. difficile infection, osteoporosis, hypomagnesaemia

### Prognosis
- **Bleeding ulcer:** mortality 5-10% (higher with age, comorbidity, rebleeding). With endoscopic treatment, rebleed rate ~10-20%.
- **Perforated ulcer:** mortality 10-30% (higher if >24h delay, sepsis). Early surgery + antibiotics improves outcome.
- **Non-complicated ulcer:** >90% heal with PPI and H. pylori eradication; recurrence <5% if successful eradication.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always check H. pylori status** in all peptic ulcers – if positive, initiate eradication therapy during the same admission (even if bleeding, provided haemodynamically stable)
- **Perform a PR exam** for melaena in any patient with epigastric pain + anaemia – a negative exam does not exclude upper GI bleed
- **If prescribed NSAID + PPI, still consider stopping the NSAID** – PPI reduces risk but does not eliminate it, especially in high-risk patients (elderly, previous ulcer, concomitant anticoagulant)

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not start empiric H. pylori treatment without confirming infection** (false positives with recent antibiotics/PPIs) and do not use antibiotics alone without PPI
- **Do not stop PPI prematurely** in bleeding ulcer: recommended 72h continuous infusion post-endoscopic haemostasis followed by oral PPI for 8 weeks
- **Do not give NSAIDs or steroids** to a patient with untreated H. pylori ulcer – may precipitate bleeding

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Black/tarry stools or red blood in vomit/stools
  - Severe abdominal pain (unrelieved by PPI, worse with movement)
  - Fever, rigors, vomiting, or inability to tolerate oral fluids/food
- **Follow-up:**
  - Outpatient OGD in 6-8 weeks if not done (or if gastric ulcer to confirm healing and exclude malignancy)
  - H. pylori eradication test at least 4 weeks after completing triple therapy (off PPI for 2 weeks) – arrange breath test or stool antigen
  - GP review if ongoing dyspepsia or alarm features

### Escalation Criteria
- **When to call senior / ICU / crash team:**
  - **NEWS2 ≥7** or rising trend with shock (HR >120, SBP <90, RR >24)
  - **Lactate >4 mmol/L** (sign of tissue hypoperfusion)
  - **GCS <13** or altered mental state
  - **Ongoing haematemesis/melaena despite resuscitation** (call ICU + on-call gastroenterology for emergency OGD)
  - **Peritonism or suspected perforation** (call surgical registrar immediately)
  - **Failed endoscopic control of bleeding** (consider interventional radiology or surgery)
